titleStmt.title: Letter from Eileen McGowan to Florence, 26 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Eileen Ellen McGowan (née O'Donovan Rossa)

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This letter, addressed to Florence, was written by Eileen McGowan (née O’Donovan Rossa), (1878 – 1963). Eileen offers to help Florence anytime she may require it, especially after all that Florence and many others in Dublin did last summer. She writes that the suffering in Ireland must be dreadful and that she would have sent money with the letter only for she had read that the British confiscate money sent in letters.Eileen was one of eighteen children of the Fenian leader Jeremiah O’Donovan Rossa (1831 – 1915). O’Donovan Rossa was an active figure in Irish politics from 1856 until 1906, until he immigrated to New York where he eventually passed away.

correspAction.placeName: 5 Arrietta Street, Tompkinsville, New York, USA

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
5 Arrietta St, Tompkinsville, N.Y. May 26thh-1916 Dear Florence:- Thanks for your
prompt acknowledgement and
I am very glad that our
good friend, Miss Murphy,
was able to assist you. What kind of work
are you doing? Rikers
and Hegamans is a good
reliable firm and there
should be chances for promotion.
Don't hesitate to write to me anytime
that I can be of service to you. It
was a small thing for me to do in
comparison with what you, and
many others in Dublin did for
us last summer. The suffering in Ireland must
be dreadful. I read in one of
the papers that the English government
will confiscate money sent across
in letters so it is useless to
try to help anyone. I wish your little family
would set a day for a visit
to me; any Saturday or Sunday
afternoon will suit me. Best wishes to your mother,
Sincerely yours, Eileen McGowan (Eileen O'Donovan Rossa)
